title: - DMSANET: DUAL MULTI SCALE ATTENTION NET WORK [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - DMSANET: DUAL MULTI SCALE ATTENTION NET WORK

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Attention mechanism of late has been quite popular in the computer vision community. A lot of work has been done to improve the performance of the network, although almost always it results in increased computational complexity. In this paper, we propose a new attention module that not only achieves the best performance but also has lesser parameters compared to most existing models. Our attention module can easily be integrated with other convolutional neural networks because of its lightweight nature. The proposed network named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) is comprised of two parts: the first part is used to extract features at various scales and aggregate them, the second part uses spatial and channel attention modules in parallel to adaptively integrate local features with their global dependencies. We benchmark our network performance for Image Classification on ImageNet dataset, Object Detection and Instance Segmentation both on MS COCO dataset.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The local receptive field of the human eye has led to the construction of convolutional neural networks which has powered much of the recent advances in computer vision. Multi scale architecture used in the famous InceptionNet (Szegedy et al., 2016) aggregates multi-scale information from different size convolutional kernels. Attention Networks has attracted a lot of attention recently as it allows the network to focus on only then essential aspects while ignoring the ones which are not useful (Li et al., 2019), (Cao et al., 2019) and (Li et al., 2019).

A lot of problems have been successfully tackled using attention mechanism in computer vision like image classification, image segmentation, object detection and image generation. Most of the attention mechanisms can be broadly classified into two types channel attention and spatial attention, both of which strengthens the original features by aggregating the same feature from all the positions with different aggregation strategies, transformations, and strengthening functions (Zhang et al., 2021).

Some of the work combined both these mechanism together and achieved better results (Cao et al., 2019) and (Woo et al., 2018). The computational burden was reduced by (Wang et al., 2020) using efficient channel attention and 1 _×_ 1 convolution. The most popular attention mechanism is the Squeeze-and Excitation module (Hu et al., 2018b), which can significantly improve the performance with a considerably low cost. The “channel shuffle” operator is used (Zhang and Yang, 2021) to enable information communication between the two branches. It uses a grouping strategy, which divides the input feature map into groups along the channel dimension.

## 2 RELATED WORK

There are two main problems which hinders the progress in this field: 1) Both spatial and channel attention as well as network using combination of two uses only local information while ignoring long range channel dependency, 2) The previous architectures fail to capture spatial information at different scales to be more robust and handle more complex problems. These two challenges were tackled by (Duta et al., 2020) and (Li et al., 2019) respectivly. The problem with these architectures is that the number of parameters increased considerably.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Pyramid Split Attention (PSA) (Zhang et al., 2021) has the ability to process the input tensor at multiple scales. A multi-scale pyramid convolution structure is used to integrate information at different scales on each channel-wise feature map. The channel-wise attention weight of the multi-scale feature maps are extracted hence long range channel dependency is done.

Non-Local block (Wang et al., 2018) is proposed to build a dense spatial feature map and capture the long-range dependency using non-local operations. (Li et al., 2019) used a dynamic selection attention mechanism that allows each neuron to adaptively adjust its receptive field size based on multiple scales of input feature map. (Fu et al., 2019) proposed a network to integrate local features with their global dependencies by summing these two attention modules from different branches.

Multi scale architectures have been used sucessfully for a lot of vision problems ( **?** ), (Hu et al., 2018b) and (Sagar and Soundrapandiyan, 2020). (Fu et al., 2019) adaptively integrated local features with their global dependencies by summing the two attention modules from different branches. (Hu et al., 2018a) used spatial extension using a depth-wise convolution to aggregate individual features. Our network borrows ideas from (Gao et al., 2018) which used a network to capture local cross-channel interactions.
The performance (in terms of accuracy) vs computational complexity (in terms of number of parameters) of the state of art attention modules is shown in Figure 1:

Figure 1: Comparing the accuracy of different attention methods with ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 as backbone in terms of accuracy and network parameters. The circles reflects the network parameters and FLOPs of different models. Our proposed network achieves higher accuracy while having less model complexity.

Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:

• A new attention module is proposed which aggregates feature information at various scales. Our network is scalable and can be easily plugged into various computer vision problems.

- Our network captures more contextual information using both spatial and channel attention at

- various scales.
• Our experiments demonstrate that our network outperforms previous state of the art with lesser computational cost.

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 FEATURE GROUPING

Shuffle Attention module divides the input feature map into groups and uses Shuffle Unit to integrate the channel attention and spatial attention into one block for each group. The sub-features are aggregated and a “channel shuffle” operator is used for communicating the information between different sub-features.

For a given feature map _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ , where _C, H, W_ indicate the channel number, spatial height, and width, respectively, shuffle attention module divides _X_ into _G_ groups along the channel dimension, i.e., _X_ = [ _X_ 1 _, XG_ ] _, Xk ∈ R[C/G][×][H][×][W]_ . An attention module is used to weight the importance of each feature. The input of _Xk_ is split into two networks along the channel dimension _Xk_ 1 _, Xk_ 2 _∈ R[C/]_[2] _[G][×][H][×][W]_ . The first branch is used to produce a channel attention map by using the relationship of channels, while the second branch is used to generate a spatial attention map by using the spatial relationship of different features.

## 3.2 CHANNEL ATTENTION MODULE

The channel attention module is used to selectively weight the importance of each channel and thus produces best output features. This helps in reducing the number of parameters of the network. Let _X ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ denotes the input feature map, where the quantity _H, W, C_ represent its height, width and number of input channels respectively. A SE block consists of two parts: squeeze and excitation, which are respectively designed for encoding the global information and adaptively recalibrating the channel-wise relationship. The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1:
The attention weight of the _c[th]_ channel in the SE block can be written as denoted in Equation 2:
where _W_ 0 _∈ R[C][×][C] r_ and _W_ 1 _∈ R[Cr][×][C]_ represent the fully-connected (FC) layers. The symbol _σ_ represents the excitation function where Sigmoid function is usually used.

We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. We then apply a softmax layer to obtain the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ as shown in Equation 3:

where _xji_ measures the _i[th]_ channel’s impact on the _j[th]_ channel. We perform a matrix multiplication between the transpose of _X_ and _A_ and reshape their result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We also multiply the result by a scale parameter _β_ and perform an element-wise sum operation with _A_ to obtain the final output _E ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ as shown in Equation 4:

## 3.3 SPATIAL ATTENTION MODULE

We use Instance Normalization (IN) over _Xk_ 2 to obtain spatial-wise statistics. A _Fc_ () operation is used to enhance the representation of _Xk_ 2. The final output of spatial attention is obtained by where

3

_W_ 2 and _b_ 2 are parameters with shape _R[C/]_[2] _[G][×]_[1] _[×]_[1] . After that the two branches are concatenated to make the number of channels equal to the number of input.

A local feature denoted by _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ is fed into a convolution layer to generate two new feature maps _B_ and _C_ , respectively where _B, C ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape them to _R[C][×][N]_ , where _N_ = _H × W_ is the number of pixels. Next a matrix multiplication is done between the transpose of _C_ and _B_ , and apply a softmax layer to calculate the spatial attention map _S ∈ R[N][×][N]_ . This operation is shown in Equation 1:

where _sji_ measures the _i[th]_ position’s impact on _j[th]_ position. Next we feed feature A into a convolution layer to generate a new feature map _D ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ and reshape it to _R[C][×][N]_ . We perform a matrix multiplication between _D_ and the transpose of _S_ and reshape the result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We multiply it by a scale parameter _α_ and perform a element-wise sum operation with the feature _A_ to obtain the final output _E ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ as shown in Equation 2:

## 3.4 AGGREGATION

In the final part of the network, all the sub-features are aggregated. We use a ”channel shuffle” operator to enable cross-group information flow along the channel dimension. The final output of our module is the same size as that of input, making our attention module quite easy to integrate with other networks.
The whole multi-scale pre-processed feature map can be obtained by a concatenation way as defined in Equation 7:

where _F ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ is the obtained multi-scale feature map. Our attention module is used across channels to adaptively select different spatial scales which is guided by the feature descriptor. This operation is defined in Equation 8:
Finally we multiply the re-calibrated weight of multi-scale channel attention _atti_ with the feature map of the corresponding scale _Fi_ as shown in Equation 9:

## 3.5 NETWORK ARCHITECTURE

We propose DMSA module with the goal to build more efficient and scalable architecture. The first part of our network borrows ideas from (Li et al., 2019) and (Zhang and Yang, 2021). An input feature map _X_ is splitted into _N_ parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has _C_ 0= _CS_ number of common channels, and the _i[th]_ feature map is _Xi ∈ R[C]_[0] _[×][H][×][W]_ . The individual features are fused before being passed to two different branches.

These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation. The second part of our network does the following 1) Builds a spatial attention matrix which models the spatial relationship between any

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

two pixels of the features, 2) A matrix multiplication between the attention matrix and the original features. 3) An element-wise sum operation is done on the resulting matrix and original features.

The operators concat and sum are used to reshape the features. The features from the two parallel branches are aggregated to produce the final output. The complete network architecture is shown in Figure 2:

Figure 2: A detailed Illustration of DMSANet

.

We compare our network architecture with Resnet (Wang et al., 2017), SENet (Hu et al., 2018b) and EPSANet (Zhang et al., 2021) in Figure 3. We use our DMSA module in between 3 _×_ 3 convolution and 1 _×_ 1 convolution. Our network is able to extract features at various scales and aggregate those individual features before passing through the attention module.

Figure 3: Illustration and comparison of ResNet, SENet, EPSANet and our proposed DMSANet blocks.

The architectural details our proposed attention network is shown in Table 1:

## 3.6 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

We use Residual Networks (He et al., 2016) as the backbone which is widely used in literature for image classification on Imagenet dataset (Deng et al., 2009). Data augmentation is used for increasing the size of the dataset and the input tensor is cropped to size 224 _×_ 224. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 1: Network design of the proposed DMSANet.

We use Residual Network along with FPN as the backbone network (Lin et al., 2017a) for object detection. The detectors we benchmark against are Faster RCNN (Ren et al., 2015), Mask RCNN(He et al., 2017) and RetinaNet (Lin et al., 2017b) on MS-COCO dataset (Lin et al., 2014). Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 16 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

For instance segmentation we use Mask RCNN (He et al., 2017) with FPN (Lin et al., 2017a) as backbone. Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with a weight decay of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum is 0.9, and the batch size is 4 per GPU for 10 epochs. The learning rate is set as 0.01 and is decreased by the factor of 10 every 10th epoch.

## 4 RESULTS

## 4.1 IMAGE CLASSIFICATION ON IMAGENET

We compare our network with previous state of the art on ResNet with 50 and 101 layers.

Our network shows the best performance in accuracy, achieving a considerable improvement compared with all the previous attention models along with lower computational cost. The comparision of our network against previous state of the art with ResNet50 as backbone is shown in Table 2:
The comparision of our network against previous state of the art with ResNet101 as backbone is shown in Table 3:

## 4.2 OBJECT DETECTION ON MS COCO

The comparision of our network using Faster RCNN detector against previous state of the art is shown in Table 4:
The comparision of our network using MASK RCNN detector against previous state of the art is shown in Table 5:
The comparision of our network using RetinaNet detector against previous state of the art is shown in Table 6:

Table 2: Comparison of various attention methods on ImageNet with ResNet50 as backbone in terms of network parameters(in millions), floating point operations per second (FLOPs), Top-1 and Top-5 Validation Accuracy(%). The best records are marked in bold.

Table 3: Comparison of various attention methods on ImageNet with ResNet 101 as backbone in terms of network parameters(in millions), floating point operations per second (FLOPs), Top-1 and Top-5 Validation Accuracy(%). The best records are marked in bold.
Table 4: Comparison of object detection results on COCO val2017 using Faster RCNN detector. The best records are marked in bold.

## 4.3 INSTANCE SEGMENTATION ON MS COCO

We used Mask-RCNN (He et al., 2017) as benchmark on MS-COCO dataset (Lin et al., 2014). The comparision results of our network on instance segmentation using MS COCO dataset against previous state of the art is shown in Table 7:

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 5: Comparison of object detection results on COCO val2017 using Mask RCNN detector. The best records are marked in bold.
Table 6: Comparison of object detection results on COCO val2017 using RetinaNet detector. The best records are marked in bold.
Table 7: Instance segmentation results of different attention networks by using the Mask R-CNN on COCO. The best records are marked in bold.

## 4.4 ABLATION STUDY

The ablation studies of our architecture is shown in Table 8. The results are best obtained using instance normalization. Both removing _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv results in reduced performance as compared to the original network. The earlier is because _Fc_ () is used to enhance the performance of individual features while latter is because number of channels in each sub-feature is too few, so it is not important to exchange information among different channels.

## 5 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we propose a novel Attention module named Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet). Our network is comprised of two parts 1) first for aggregating feature information at various scales 2) second made up of position and channel attention modules in parallel for capturing global contextual information. After evaluating our network both qualitatively and quantitatively, we show that our network outperforms previous state of the art across image classification, object

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 8: Performance comparisons of our network using ResNet 50 as backbone with four options (i.e., original, using Batch Normalization, using Group Normalization, using shuffle normalization, eliminating _Fc_ () and using 1 _×_ 1 Conv to replace _Fc_ () on ImageNet-1k in terms of GFLOPs and Top-1/Top-5 accuracy (in %). The best records are marked in bold.

detection and instance segmentation problems. The ablation experiments show that our attention module captures long-range contextual information effectively at various scales thus making it generalizable to other tasks. The best part of DMSANet attention module is that it is very lightweight and hence could be easily plugged into various custom networks as and when required.

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

We would like to thank Nvidia for providing the GPUs for this work.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces DMSANet, a dual multi-scale attention module designed to improve vision tasks like classification and detection with lower computational overhead. While the reported experimental results are impressive, the technical description of the architecture is significantly flawed. The 'Method' section is a fragmented compilation of descriptions from different existing papers (Shuffle Attention and Dual Attention Network), leading to contradictory formulas and a complete lack of clarity on how 'Multi-Scale' features are actually generated or integrated. Key equations are missing or mislabeled, and the relationship between the descriptive text and the provided figures is weak, making the proposed method currently irreproducible.

comments.title: Duplicated or Misplaced Equation Labels
comments.quote: The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1: [Blank/Missing] ... Next a matrix multiplication is done ... apply a softmax layer to calculate the spatial attention map S. This operation is shown in Equation 1: [Equation 5 shown]
comments.explanation: The text references 'Equation 1' twice for completely different operations. In Section 3.2, it refers to it for Global Average Pooling, but no equation is printed there. In Section 3.3, it denotes a spatial attention formula as Equation 1, even though it follows Equation 4. This makes the formal derivation of the model impossible to follow.

comments.title: Contradictory Architecture Descriptions
comments.quote: Section 3.1: 'shuffle attention module divides X into G groups... split into two networks... Xk1, Xk2' vs Section 3.2/3.3: references local features 'A' and 'B, C, D' and matrix multiplications of size CxC.
comments.explanation: Section 3.1 describes a 'Shuffle Attention' style grouping mechanism (from Zhang & Yang 2021). However, Sections 3.2 and 3.3 describe a 'Dual Attention' mechanism (from Fu et al. 2019) using matrix multiplications on RxN manifolds. These are two distinct architectural paradigms. The paper does not explain if they are used sequentially, in parallel, or if one is a sub-component of the other. The notation 'Xk1' in Section 3.1 is never used in the actual equations in 3.2/3.3, which use 'A'.

comments.title: Unspecified Multi-Scale Mechanism
comments.quote: The whole multi-scale pre-processed feature map can be obtained by a concatenation way as defined in Equation 7: [Equation 7 is missing or not provided clearly]
comments.explanation: The 'Multi-Scale' aspect is a core claim of the paper (DMSANet). However, there is no formal description of how these scales are generated (e.g., varying kernel sizes or dilated convolutions). Equation 7 is mentioned as defining the concatenation of multi-scale features, but it is absent from the text. Without this, the 'Multi-Scale' part of the name is unsupported by the methodology.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Text and Tables
comments.quote: 3.6 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS: 'The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.'
comments.explanation: The text states the model was trained for 50 epochs. For ImageNet-1k, standard practice is typically 90-120 epochs. Training for only 50 epochs generally results in lower accuracy than state-of-the-art. Yet, Table 2 shows the model significantly outperforming models like ResNet and SENet (which are typically trained for longer). This creates a doubt regarding the fairness of the comparison or the accuracy of the implementation details provided.

comments.title: Ambiguous Scale Parameters
comments.quote: We also multiply the result by a scale parameter $\beta$ and perform an element-wise sum (Eq 4) ... multiply it by a scale parameter $\alpha$ (Eq 2 in 3.3).
comments.explanation: The authors introduce $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the context of global dependencies. It is unclear if these are learnable scalars (as in the original Non-Local/DANet papers) or fixed hyperparameters. Given the claim of 'lesser parameters,' the initialization and nature of these parameters should be specified.

comments.title: Variable Substitution Confusion (OCR or Author error)
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map $X \in R^{C \times C}$ ... reshape A to $R^{C \times N}$ ... Element-wise sum operation with A to obtain final output $E$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, 'X' is defined initially as the input feature map ($R^{C 	imes H 	imes W}$), but later in the same section, 'X' is defined as the channel attention map ($R^{C 	imes C}$). Using the same symbol for a 3D tensor and a 2D weighting matrix within the same sub-section is highly misleading for the reader.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes DMSANet, a dual multi-scale attention network that aims to improve performance while reducing computational complexity. The overall idea of combining multi-scale feature aggregation with parallel spatial and channel attention is sound and aligns with recent trends in efficient attention design. The authors provide comprehensive experimental validation across multiple vision tasks and demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines. However, several technical issues in the formulation and presentation—particularly around inconsistent notation, undefined equations, and missing derivations—raise concerns about reproducibility and correctness. While the empirical results appear promising, the methodological description requires clarification to ensure the claims are fully supportable.

Next a matrix multiplication is done between the transpose of _C_ and _B_ , and apply a softmax layer to calculate the spatial attention map _S ∈ R[N][×][N]_ . This operation is shown in Equation 1: 

where _sji_ measures the _i[th]_ position’s impact on _j[th]_ position. Next we feed feature A into a convolution layer to generate a new feature map _D ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ and reshape it to _R[C][×][N]_ . We perform a matrix multiplication between _D_ and the transpose of _S_ and reshape the result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We multiply it by a scale parameter _α_ and perform a element-wise sum operation with the feature _A_ to obtain the final output _E ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: I was immediately concerned that Equations 1–4 and the second Equation 1–2 are referenced but not displayed—they are entirely missing from the text. The surrounding prose describes operations that resemble non-local blocks or self-attention (e.g., computing attention maps via matrix multiplication and softmax), yet without the actual equations, it is impossible to verify correctness or understand key details like normalization, scaling, or indexing. The description suggests use of a dot-product attention mechanism, but the absence of formal expressions makes the method underspecified. While some formulations are standard in the literature, the authors are attempting a novel integration and should present their full formulation. This issue persists across both channel and spatial attention descriptions and is not resolved by context or forward references.

comments.title: Incorrect output dimension in channel attention formulation
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A. We then apply a softmax layer to obtain the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ as shown in Equation 3:
comments.explanation: The claim that the channel attention map is in _R[C][×][C]_ after computing A @ A^T (where A is reshaped to _R[C][×][N]_) is mathematically correct—this yields _R[C][×][C]_. However, interpreting such a map as modeling pairwise channel dependencies and then applying it via multiplication with A is atypical. Standard squeeze-and-excitation produces a _R[C]_ vector, not a _R[C][×][C]_ matrix. While non-local-style channel attention exists (e.g., in GCNet), the authors do not clarify whether this is a self-attention variant or a misstatement. The subsequent step—multiplying the transpose of _X_ with _A_—would require careful indexing. The operation as described (X^T @ A_reshaped) would yield _R[C][×][N]_, which reshapes to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_, but this contradicts Equation 4’s implication of element-wise scaling. Thus, there is a likely inconsistency between the text and the intended operation: either the attention is multiplicative (like SE) or transformative (like non-local). The prose suggests the latter, but the integration with the residual pathway blurs this.

comments.title: Inconsistent and undefined use of 'Fc()' operation
comments.quote:
A _Fc_ () operation is used to enhance the representation of _Xk_ 2. The final output of spatial attention is obtained by where 

_W_ 2 and _b_ 2 are parameters with shape _R[C/]_[2] _[G][×]_[1] _[×]_[1] .
comments.explanation: The sentence 'The final output of spatial attention is obtained by where' is grammatically incomplete and cuts off, suggesting a missing formula. It references an _Fc_() operation whose purpose and structure are unclear. From context, _Fc_() may refer to a fully connected layer, but applied across what dimensions? The parameters _W_2 and _b_2 are defined with a shape _R[C/(2G) × 1 × 1]_, which suggests a 1×1 convolution, not an FC layer. This is a likely confusion in terminology. In efficient attention modules, 1×1 convolutions often replace FC layers for spatial dimensions. However, calling this 'Fc()' misleads readers into expecting a global operation. The ablation study in Table 8 confirms this is critical, but the distinction between _Fc_() and 1×1 Conv is blurred. If _Fc_() is merely a 1×1 Conv, the ablation 'using 1×1 Conv to replace _Fc_()' becomes nonsensical.

comments.title: Mismatch between network description and Table 1 architecture
comments.explanation: Table 1 attempts to compare ResNet-50 and DMSANet blocks, but the notation is garbled—likely due to OCR error. Symbols like '' suggest lost characters. More critically, the ResNet-50 block shows three convolutions (1×1, 3×3, 1×1) with channels 64, 64, 256, matching a standard bottleneck. The DMSANet row shows '1×1, DMSA, 1×1' with the same channel sizes. However, the DMSA module is described as replacing or augmenting the attention inside the block, not as an atomic unit between convolutions. The text says 'we use our DMSA module in between 3×3 convolution and 1×1 convolution'—this placement is ambiguous. Is DMSA applied after the 3×3 conv and before the final 1×1? If so, the input to DMSA would be 64 channels, but DMSA involves splitting channels, which may not be feasible at that size. The table fails to clarify the architectural insertion point or whether DMSA replaces or supplements existing layers.

comments.title: Learning rate inconsistency in implementation details
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 _e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The text first states the learning rate is 1e-4, but then says it is initially set to 0.1. These are contradictory. Given standard ImageNet training protocols, 0.1 is the typical initial learning rate for SGD with batch size 256, scaled down for smaller batches. The batch size here is 64, so 0.1 would be too high unless warmup is used. The ablation results (e.g., 80.02% top-1) suggest proper training, so 1e-4 is likely a typo. However, the sentence structure implies both values are stated as fact. This numerical inconsistency could mislead replication attempts. While readers may infer the 0.1 value is correct due to common practice, the conflict remains unresolved in the text.

comments.title: Misleading parameter count comparison in Figure 1
comments.quote:
The performance (in terms of accuracy) vs computational complexity (in terms of number of parameters) of the state of art attention modules is shown in Figure 1: 

Figure 1: Comparing the accuracy of different attention methods with ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 as backbone in terms of accuracy and network parameters. The circles reflects the network parameters and FLOPs of different models. Our proposed network achieves higher accuracy while having less model complexity.
comments.explanation: Figure 1 is referenced but not included in the submission (likely excluded under double-blind policy). However, the caption claims it shows both parameters and FLOPs, yet the title only mentions parameters. More importantly, the claim that DMSANet has 'less model complexity' is supported in Table 2 (26.25M vs 28.07M for CBAM) for ResNet-50, but in Table 3 (ResNet-101), DMSANet has 42.29M vs EPSANet(Small)'s 38.90M. Thus, it does not universally have fewer parameters. The statement overreaches by implying a uniform reduction across backbones. While it achieves higher accuracy, the complexity claim is qualified. The abstract makes a similar claim: 'lesser parameters compared to most existing models' — 'most' saves it, but Figure 1's caption is stronger.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper proposes DMSANet, a dual multi-scale attention network for computer vision tasks. The architecture combines multi-scale feature extraction with parallel spatial and channel attention modules. While the concept is interesting and the reported results are impressive (2-3% improvements over SOTA on ImageNet), there are several technical issues that undermine the paper's credibility and reproducibility. The most concerning aspect is the unusually large performance gains compared to existing attention modules, which typically provide 1-2% improvements at most. Additionally, there are inconsistencies in notation, undefined operations, and contradictory implementation details that make it difficult to reproduce the results exactly.

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rate specification
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1 e[−]_[4] , momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: The text first states the learning rate is 1e-4, then immediately contradicts this by saying it's initially set to 0.1. This is a significant discrepancy as 0.1 is 1000x larger than 1e-4. I checked if this might be a typo or if one refers to a different experiment, but both statements appear in the same sentence describing the ImageNet training setup. This inconsistency affects reproducibility as the learning rate is a critical hyperparameter.

comments.title: Unclear channel attention implementation
comments.quote: The attention weight of the _c[th]_ channel in the SE block can be written as denoted in Equation 2: ... We calculate the channel attention map _X ∈ R[C][×][C]_ from the original features _A ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ . We reshape A to _R[C][×][N]_ , and then perform a matrix multiplication between A and the transpose of A.
comments.explanation: The paper first describes the standard SE block mechanism in Equation 2, which uses FC layers to compute channel attention weights. Then it describes a completely different approach using self-attention between channels (matrix multiplication of reshaped features). It's unclear which method is actually used in DMSANet, or if both are used in some combination. This ambiguity makes it impossible to understand the exact channel attention mechanism being proposed.

comments.title: Vague attention operation in aggregation
comments.quote: This operation is defined in Equation 8: _atti_ = _Attention_(_Fi_)
comments.explanation: Equation 8 uses an undefined 'Attention' function without specifying what type of attention or how it's computed. Given that the paper has already described multiple attention mechanisms (channel, spatial), this is critically underspecified. I checked if this might refer to a previously defined operation, but no such definition exists in the context. This prevents understanding how the multi-scale features are actually reweighted.

comments.title: Questionable performance gains
comments.explanation: The reported 80.02% Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet with ResNet-50 backbone represents a 1.5% improvement over the previous best (FcaNet at 78.52%). Most attention modules typically provide 1-2% improvements at best, with many providing less than 1%. A 1.5% jump is unusually large and raises questions about the validity of the results. While the ablation study shows the full method performs best, the magnitude of improvement over established methods seems suspicious and may indicate potential issues with experimental setup or reporting.

comments.title: Undefined Fc() operation
comments.quote: We use Instance Normalization (IN) over _Xk_ 2 to obtain spatial-wise statistics. A _Fc_ () operation is used to enhance the representation of _Xk_ 2.
comments.explanation: The operation Fc() is mentioned but never defined. Based on context, it likely refers to a fully connected layer, but this is not explicitly stated. The notation is also inconsistent with the standard FC notation used elsewhere in the paper (W_0, W_1 in Equation 2). While this might seem minor, it affects the precise understanding of the spatial attention module's implementation.

comments.title: Equation numbering inconsistency
comments.quote: This operation is shown in Equation 1: ... as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3, the spatial attention equations are labeled as 'Equation 1' and 'Equation 2', but these are different from the equations numbered 1 and 2 in Section 3.2. This creates confusion when referencing specific equations. While the equations themselves are mathematically sound, the inconsistent numbering makes it difficult to follow the technical development and cross-reference between sections.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to attention mechanisms in CNNs by combining multi-scale feature extraction with dual spatial and channel attention pathways. While the empirical results are strong, the paper suffers from significant technical documentation issues, including missing equation definitions, inconsistent referencing of equations, and ambiguous notation. These gaps, particularly in the mathematical description of the core modules, make it difficult to independently verify the underlying mechanics of the proposed DMSANet.

comments.title: Missing Formula Definitions
comments.quote: The Global Average Pooling (GAP) operation can be calculated by the as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The text refers to "Equation 1" for the definition of Global Average Pooling, but the equation itself is missing from the document. The subsequent text jumps to Equation 2, leaving the calculation of GAP undefined, which is a fundamental building block of the channel attention module.

comments.title: Equation Referencing Errors
comments.quote: This operation is shown in Equation 1: ... We multiply it by a scale parameter \( \alpha \) and perform a element-wise sum operation with the feature \( A \) to obtain the final output \( E \in R[C][\times][H][\times][W] \) as shown in Equation 2:
comments.explanation: In Section 3.3 (Spatial Attention Module), the authors refer to Equation 1 and Equation 2 to describe the spatial attention calculation. However, these references follow Equation 4 in Section 3.2. These references likely point to equations within the current section, but they are mislabeled as 1 and 2, creating confusion about which specific transformation is being referenced.

comments.title: Notation Ambiguity in Feature Splitting
comments.quote: An input feature map \( X \) is splitted into \( N \) parts along with the channel dimension. For each splitted parts, it has \( C_0 = C/S \) number of common channels
comments.explanation: The variable \( S \) is introduced here without prior definition. In previous sections, \( G \) was used to denote the number of groups (divisions of channels). It is unclear if \( S \) is a typo for \( G \) or a new hyperparameter, making the channel dimension calculation ambiguous.

comments.title: Undefined Symbol in Equation
comments.quote: where \( W_0 \in R[C][\times][C/r] \) and \( W_1 \in R[C/r][\times][C] \) represent the fully-connected (FC) layers.
comments.explanation: The symbol \( r \) is utilized to define the shapes of the weight matrices in the channel attention module, but its value and purpose (likely a reduction ratio) are never defined or discussed in the text.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a dual multi-scale attention network (DMSANet) and reports strong performance on ImageNet and MS-COCO benchmarks. However, the manuscript contains critical omissions and contradictions that significantly hinder reproducibility and clarity. Most notably, several core equations are missing entirely, likely due to OCR failures, leaving the attention mechanisms mathematically undefined. There are also direct contradictions in implementation details (learning rate values), notational overloading that confuses feature maps and attention matrices, and mismatches between ablation text and tables. While the experimental claims appear promising, these technical gaps must be resolved before the work can be properly evaluated or reproduced.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel attention module that claims state-of-the-art results on image classification, object detection, and instance segmentation with lower computational cost. However, the manuscript suffers from several critical issues that undermine its clarity and reproducibility. The most severe problem is the missing equations referenced throughout the methodology (e.g., equations for channel and spatial attention, aggregation formulas), which prevents readers from understanding the exact operations. Additionally, there are inconsistencies in the reported learning rate for ImageNet training, unusually large performance gains over strong baselines without explanation, potential errors in FLOPs counting, and underspecified design choices for the multi-scale aggregation. These issues need to be addressed before the paper can be accepted.

comments.title: Missing equations for attention modules
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map X ∈ R^{C×C} from the original features A ∈ R^{C×H×W} ... as shown in Equation 3: ... as shown in Equation 4:
comments.explanation: The paper repeatedly refers to equations (e.g., Equation 1, 2, 3, 4 in Section 3.2 and Section 3.3) that are not present in the text. Without these equations, the precise definitions of the channel and spatial attention maps—including the softmax normalization, matrix multiplications, and scaling factors—are left to the reader's interpretation. Since the paper claims a new attention module, omitting the core mathematical formulations makes the methodology non-reproducible and is a fundamental flaw.

comments.title: Inconsistent learning rate in ImageNet implementation
comments.quote: Stochastic Gradient Descent is used as the optimizer with learning rate of 1e−4, momentum as 0.9 and mini batch size of 64. The learning rate is initially set as 0.1 and is decreased by a factor of 10 after every 20 epochs for 50 epochs in total.
comments.explanation: Two different learning rates are stated without clarification. The first sentence says the learning rate is 1e−4, but the second says it is initially 0.1 and then decayed. It is unclear which value was actually used, and this contradiction directly affects reproducibility. A possible reading is that the first '1e−4' is a typo and should be '0.1', but the paper does not confirm this.

comments.title: Unusually large accuracy improvements on ImageNet
comments.quote: DMSANet ... Top-1 80.02% ... EPSANet(Large) ... 78.64% ... (Table 2)
comments.explanation: DMSANet outperforms EPSANet(Large) by 1.38% on ResNet-50 and by 2.16% on ResNet-101. These gains are substantially larger than typical improvements from attention modules (usually <1%). The paper does not provide any analysis of why DMSANet achieves such a large margin, nor does it report training variations, test-time augmentation, or confidence intervals. This raises concerns about fair comparison or potential overfitting. The claim of state-of-the-art would require stronger evidence.

comments.title: Notation overload for variable X
comments.quote: We calculate the channel attention map X ∈ R^{C×C} from the original features A ∈ R^{C×H×W}.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, X is defined as the input feature map (X ∈ R^{C×H×W}). In Section 3.2, X is re‑used to denote the channel attention map (a C×C matrix). This overloading could confuse readers, especially since both symbols appear in the same context. The paper should use distinct symbols (e.g., M for the attention map).

comments.title: Underspecified multi‑scale feature aggregation
comments.quote: The whole multi-scale pre-processed feature map can be obtained by a concatenation way as defined in Equation 7: ... This operation is defined in Equation 8: ... as shown in Equation 9:
comments.explanation: Equations 7–9 are not provided, so the precise method of concatenating multi‑scale features and re‑calibrating them with attention weights is unclear. Moreover, the text does not specify how many scales are used, what convolution kernels define each scale, or how the attention weights att_i are computed. Without these details, the aggregation step cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Suspiciously low FLOPs for DMSANet compared to baseline
comments.quote: DMSANet ... FLOPs 3.44G (Table 2) ... ResNet ... FLOPs 4.12G
comments.explanation: DMSANet uses additional attention modules but reports lower FLOPs than the plain ResNet‑50 backbone. Typically, adding attention (e.g., SENet, CBAM) increases FLOPs slightly (to ~4.13G). DMSANet’s FLOPs (3.44G) is 16% lower than ResNet’s, which is counterintuitive and may indicate a calculation error. The paper does not explain how this reduction is achieved, and no FLOPs breakdown is provided.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a Dual Multi Scale Attention Network (DMSANet) claiming state-of-the-art performance with lower computational cost. However, the review identifies several critical issues. First, the mathematical derivation of the spatial attention module is dimensionally confusing and computationally implausible for an 'efficient' model. Second, the claims regarding parameter efficiency are factually inconsistent when compared to the 'Small' variants of state-of-the-art competitors in the results tables. Third, the ablation study data shows an illogical increase in FLOPs when removing parameters. Finally, the description of the network architecture is copied verbatim from a related work citation without sufficient adaptation or explanation of the novel contributions.

comments.title: Inconsistent derivation of spatial attention matrix dimensions
comments.quote: where _sji_ measures the _i[th]_ position’s impact on _j[th]_ position. Next we feed feature A into a convolution layer to generate a new feature map _D ∈ R[C][×][H][×][W]_ and reshape it to _R[C][×][N]_ . We perform a matrix multiplication between _D_ and the transpose of _S_ and reshape the result to _R[C][×][H][×][W]_ .
comments.explanation: In section 3.3, the authors describe generating a spatial attention map S ∈ R[N][×][N], where N = H × W (number of pixels). While mathematically possible to multiply a feature map D (C × N) by S^T (N × N), producing an N × N attention matrix implies calculating interactions between *every pair of pixels*, which contradicts standard 'Spatial Attention' definitions (which typically produce a scalar per position, H × W). Furthermore, O(N²) operations would be prohibitive for a lightweight model claiming only 3.44G FLOPs on ImageNet. The mechanism described seems closer to a non-local block or cross-attention, but is conflated with spatial attention here.

comments.title: Parameter efficiency claim contradicts experimental results
comments.quote: Our attention module not only achieves the best performance but also has lesser parameters compared to most existing models.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims 'lesser parameters compared to most existing models.' However, Table 2 (ImageNet Classification, ResNet-50 backbone) shows that EPSANet(Small) has 22.56M parameters, while DMSANet has 26.25M. EPSANet(Small) is a state-of-the-art variant in the same comparison table. The claim holds true only against 'Large' variants of existing models (e.g., EPSANet(Large)), but is factually false against the 'Small' variants. The claim should be refined to explicitly compare against the Small variants or the full backbone.

comments.title: Inconsistent ablation study FLOPs data
comments.quote: Methods| GFLOPs... origin|**3.44**... W/O_Fc_()|4.07
comments.explanation: The ablation study table shows that removing the _Fc_() module increases the FLOPs from 3.44 to 4.07. Since Fc layers consist of matrix multiplications, removing them should theoretically reduce computational complexity, not increase it. This data inconsistency suggests a potential error in the baseline measurement, the definition of the 'W/O_Fc' variant, or a miscalculation of the FLOPs.

comments.title: Incorrect operation description in architecture description
comments.quote: The operators concat and sum are used to reshape the features.
comments.explanation: The text in section 3.5 states that 'concat and sum are used to reshape the features.' Concatenation (stacking tensors along a dimension) and summation (adding tensors element-wise) do not 'reshape' features in the technical sense; concatenation changes the number of channels, and summation preserves channel depth. This description is mathematically vague and potentially misleading regarding how the feature tensors are transformed.

comments.title: Architecture description copied from related work
comments.quote: These two branches are comprised of position attention module and channel attention module as proposed in (Fu et al., 2019) for semantic segmentation. The second part of our network does the following 1) Builds a spatial attention matrix which models the spatial relationship between any two pixels of the features, 2) A matrix multiplication between the attention matrix and the original features. 3) An element-wise sum operation is done on the resulting matrix and original features.
comments.explanation: The paragraph describing the network architecture in section 3.5 is essentially a verbatim copy of the explanation for (Fu et al., 2019) found in the Related Work section. While the authors state they 'borrow ideas from' (Gao et al., 2018) and (Li et al., 2019), the detailed architecture mechanism described seems to be lifted directly from the Fu et al. reference. This fails to clearly articulate what is novel or unique about DMSANet's specific architectural components.

comments.title: OCR misread in tensor notation
comments.explanation: The OCR text for the weight tensor shape in Equation 2 is malformed as '_R[C][×][C] r_'. The character 'r' at the end appears to be an OCR error for a reduction factor (common in SE-blocks, typically $C/r$). The current notation describes a square matrix $W_0$ of size $C 	imes C$, which is likely incorrect for a channel attention weight map (usually smaller due to reduction).
